header.tagline: Reading the Pacific through its data

home.subtitle: A dive into Pacific data: a sea that rises, that warms, and the peoples who carry its weight.
home.live_loading: Connecting to the Pacific Data Hub…
home.acts_intro: Eleven acts, a region in action.

acts.a1_text: The Pacific emits a tiny fraction of global gases. Understanding that gap lays the first stone of a fair response.
acts.a2_text: The level rises, the surface warms. The Pacific measures it precisely — the first step to anticipate and protect.
acts.a3_text: Where land meets water, communities organise and plan their adaptation.
acts.a4_text: Behind the numbers, people — and a regional solidarity taking shape in the face of disasters.
acts.a5_text: The Pacific does not merely endure: the rise of clean energy traces a path forward.
acts.a6_text: Crops and livestock: how the islands adapt their farming to keep food security.
acts.a7_text: Red List and fisheries management: biodiversity under pressure, and the concrete efforts to preserve it.
acts.a8_text: Rainfall, temperatures, monitoring network: observing the atmosphere better to prepare for it better.
acts.a9_text: Tourism, electricity, green taxation: the economic levers of a sustainable transition.
acts.a10_text: Access to drinking water and health: tangible progress closest to people.
acts.a11_text: Everything crossed: responsibility and vulnerability, to light where the Pacific is advancing and where to focus effort.

home.thesis: The Pacific's climate story in eleven acts: not a fate, but a region that measures, adapts and acts — told through its own data.
home.shock_text: of global emissions, yet one of the loudest voices for climate. The Pacific turns its vulnerability into leadership.
home.acts_lead: From emissions to solutions, each act crosses the Pacific's open data to show a reality — and the response rising to meet it. Follow the thread: it leads to action.
home.closing: “Where others see a threat, the Pacific is already charting solutions.”
home.c1_desc: Reading what the raw numbers say: what the Pacific emits, what the ocean sends back, the human challenge taking shape. Clear facts, the basis of every solution.
home.c2_desc: Understanding the response behind the data: energy, agriculture, biodiversity, atmosphere, economy. Five fronts where the Pacific innovates and adapts.
home.c3_desc: Reading the future closest to people — water, health — then the synthesis that lights the way: what's advancing, and where to focus effort.
home.stat4_text: to read a region that adapts

footer.tagline: Giving a face to climate-change data across the Pacific.
footer.scope: Scope: all Pacific Island countries and territories (SPC).
footer.credit: Built for the Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026.

act1.lead: Pacific nations account for a tiny share of global greenhouse-gas emissions. Yet they are among the first exposed to their consequences. Here are per-capita emissions, territory by territory.
act1.chart_sub: by territory · hover a dot for details
act1.caption: Source: Pacific Data Hub (.Stat) — SPC. Per-capita values.
act1.paradox: Most Pacific territories emit little per capita, and their share of global emissions is tiny. Yet they are among the first exposed to rising seas and warming.
act1.empty: No usable data for this dataset.
act1.map_no_token: Map unavailable: add your Mapbox token to a .env file (REACT_APP_MAPBOX_TOKEN=…) at the project root, then restart “npm start”.
act1.viz_rank_sub: bars reorder by year — who rises, who falls
act1.viz_trend_sub: evolution of the biggest emitters · the cursor tracks the year

guide.title: How to read this
guide.intro: This act compares per-capita GHG emissions across Pacific territories, set against the climate impact they endure.

steps.v: The subregion pills (Melanesia / Polynesia / Micronesia) refocus every per-territory view. "All" keeps the 17.
Each bar is a territory (SPC code). Longer = more per capita. The scale is compressed to stay readable despite extremes (Palau, New Caledonia).
The line marks the Pacific MEDIAN — more robust than a mean skewed by a few outliers.
The scatter crosses what a territory emits (x) with what it endures (y): most emit little yet are highly exposed.

guide.takeaway: The paradox: emitting little, yet first to face climate change.

evo.title: Evolution over time
evo.sub: change between the first and last available years
evo.no_data: Not enough history to analyse the evolution.

act1.outlier_note: sit off the scale: tiny population denominators, diesel electricity and industry. Yet their combined share of global emissions stays negligible.

subregion.title: Average by subregion
subregion.sub: average trajectory of the three Pacific subregions

change.title: Biggest movers since
change.sub: change in per-capita emissions between first and last available year

scatter.title: Emit little, suffer most
scatter.sub: each bubble = a territory · left = low emitter · higher = more exposed
scatter.empty: Per-territory impact data unavailable — run "node scripts/fetch-pdh.mjs" or retry.

heatmap.title: The big picture
heatmap.sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = emission intensity

act2.lead: Two silent signals are redrawing the Pacific's horizon: rising sea level and warming surface temperature. Here is the retrieved data, territory by territory.
act2.coming: Data retrieved ✓ — visualisations in preparation. The full page comes next.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: Two indicators, one story: the Pacific ocean is rising and warming. Both curves show ANOMALIES — the gap from a reference period, not an absolute value.

steps.v: 0 = the reference normal. Above 0: warmer / higher than usual; below: cooler / lower.
The thick line is the average of the 21 territories; the light band shows the gap between the lowest and highest territory that year.
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on both curves AND the map at once.
Blue = below reference, red = above; circle size reflects magnitude. Pick the metric with the toggle.

guide.takeaway: Slow but unmistakable, both curves cross the reference and never come back: the Pacific is warming and the sea is rising.

act2.evo_sub: change between first and last available years
act2.heatmap_sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = deviation from normal
act2.change_sub: difference between the first and last available years

act3.lead: As the sea advances, the human map is redrawn. Population growth is slowing almost everywhere — and several islands are already losing inhabitants, while others crowd a growing population onto a threatened shore.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: The indicator is an annual population GROWTH RATE (in %), not a headcount. 0 = stable population; above 0: the island is gaining inhabitants; below: it is losing them.

steps.v: Each value is the one-year population change, in %. Negative = decline (emigration), positive = growth.
The thick line is the average of the 22 territories; the band shows where 8 in 10 territories sit (10th–90th percentile), so it isn't crushed by tiny islands with extreme swings.
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on the curve AND the 3D map at once.
Height ∝ rate magnitude; red = the island is growing (coastal pressure), blue = it is emptying (departures).

guide.takeaway: Growth is running out of steam: many islands plateau or empty out, while a handful pack ever more people onto increasingly exposed coasts.

act3.evo_sub: change in rate between first and last year

act4.timeline_sub: one circle per event, sized by magnitude

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: Here the data isn't a steady trend but a series of DISASTERS: isolated spikes, many years with no event. So we look at totals and shocks, not a curve.

steps.v: Each disaster is a dated event: people affected and economic losses cluster in a few years.
Each circle is an event placed on its year; the bigger it is, the more people it affected (or the more it cost). Clusters reveal the dark years.
The total over the whole period shows which territories carry the heaviest toll.
Heights are on a logarithmic scale: without it, Fiji would dwarf everyone else. Taller and redder means a higher total.

guide.takeaway: A handful of events — Winston (Fiji, 2016), Pam (Vanuatu, 2015) — concentrate most of the human cost: hundreds of thousands of people affected and hundreds of millions of dollars in losses.

act4.lead: Anomalies always end up with a face: people affected and economic losses, concentrated in a few disaster years.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: The final act, and it looks ahead: the share of renewables in final energy consumption (%). Here, higher is better.

steps.v: 0% = all fossil; 100% = fully renewable energy. The bottom reference (0) is the starting point, not a normal.
The thick line is the regional average; the band shows where 8 in 10 territories sit (10th–90th percentile).
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on the curve AND the green 3D map.
The taller and greener a column, the higher the renewable share. The podium ranks the year's champions.

guide.takeaway: The momentum is real: driven by hydro, biomass and now solar, the renewable share is climbing — Kiribati, Fiji, Vanuatu, Samoa and the larger Melanesian islands leading the way.

act5.evo_sub: change in share between first and last year
act5.lead: The Pacific does not suffer in silence. Against rising seas and the human cost, a trajectory is taking hold: the share of renewable energy is climbing, territory by territory.

act6.title: The food-growing land under pressure
act6.lead: As the ocean rises and warms, the Pacific's plate wobbles too. Crop yields, territory by territory — what the region harvests, and how it shifts.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: We look at agricultural yield (kg harvested per hectare), summarised per territory as the MEDIAN across every crop tracked by the Pacific Data Hub — read with care (see the caveat below).

steps.v: Everything is in kg per hectare: the land's productivity, not total output.
Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia — to compare neighbouring territories.
The line shows each territory over time; the heatmap sums it all up on a colour grid.
The explorer drills down to each product (banana, taro, coconut…) via the icons.

guide.takeaway: Yields move — sometimes sharply — but rarely for a single reason. Climate is one factor among many.

act6.sub1_sub: Median yield per territory, across all crops — computed from real Pacific Data Hub data.
act6.heatmap_sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = yield
act6.change_sub: difference between the first and last available years
act6.explorer_lead: Pick a product to see its yield by territory and the most recent ranking.
act6.caveat_body: Agricultural yield does not depend on climate alone: farming practices, inputs, prices, planted area, pests and the estimation method weigh at least as much. Several series are also partly modelled (constant values) or have gaps. So we present this data as 'food systems under pressure', not as proof of a direct climate cause-and-effect.
act6.next: Coming next: livestock yield, then climate × yield cross-analysis.
act6.unavailable: Agricultural data momentarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub. Try again in a moment.
act6.trend_sub: One mini-chart per territory · each with its own scale (read the trend, not the level) · 1990–2024
act6.crop_rank_sub: Median yield per crop across the displayed territories (kg/ha) — moves with the timeline
act6.regional_sub: Median yield across all territories, year by year (kg/ha)
act6.diversity_sub: Number of distinct crops tracked per territory — higher means a more varied agricultural basket
act6.volatility_sub: Coefficient of variation of yields over time, by territory — higher means more exposed to shocks
act6.compare_sub: Median yield per territory between two chosen years — the bar links both values
act6.sub2_sub: Median yield per animal (kg/animal), territory by territory — from the same disaggregated dataset
act6.animal_rank_sub: Median yield per animal across the displayed territories (kg/animal)
act6.explorer_animal_lead: Pick an animal to see its yield by territory and the latest ranking.

act7.title: The living world retreats
act7.lead: As species fade, territories step up management measures. Two opposing curves: loss and response.
act7.redlist_sub: Red List Index (IUCN/BirdLife): 1 = pristine, 0 = all species extinct. It tracks aggregate extinction risk and declines across nearly every territory. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.fish_sub: Number of fisheries management measures in place (multilateral and bilateral agreements), cumulative per territory — steadily rising. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.unavailable: Living-world data temporarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub.
act7.rl_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of the index by territory and year ("trend" mode: each row on its own scale).
act7.rl_rank_sub: From least to most threatened. The lower the index, the higher the extinction risk.
act7.rl_compare_sub: Change in the index over three decades. ▼ = worsening (biodiversity loss).
act7.fish_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of measures in place by territory and year.
act7.fish_rank_sub: From most to least equipped. Larger Melanesia leads, smaller territories follow.
act7.fish_compare_sub: Growth in the number of measures since 2000. ▲ = stronger management.
act7.regional_rl_sub: Median Red List Index across visible territories, year by year. The slope falls: extinction risk worsens.
act7.regional_fish_sub: Median number of management measures in place across visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: governance strengthens.

act8.title: The sky goes off-balance
act8.lead: The sea is rising — but the air and the rain are derailing too. Precipitation and temperature anomalies over Pacific lands, and the response: the station network that watches the sky.
act8.unavailable: Sky data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act8.credit: Sources: precipitation GPCP v2.3 (NOAA) · temperature NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 · network WMO/OSCAR (CC BY-SA 4.0). Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act8.rain_sub: Annual precipitation anomaly (mm) against the 1991–2020 normal. 0 = normal · negative = drier year · positive = wetter year.
act8.regional_rain_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The band shows how much islands diverge; 0 = the 1991–2020 normal.
act8.rain_hm_sub: Departure from the 1991–2020 normal, territory × year.
act8.rain_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.temp_sub: Land surface temperature anomaly (°C, at 2 m) against the 1971–2000 normal. 0 = normal · negative = cooler · positive = warmer.
act8.regional_temp_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: the land surface is warming; 0 = the 1971–2000 normal.
act8.temp_hm_sub: Departure from the 1971–2000 normal, territory × year.
act8.temp_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.meteo_sub: Number of operational weather stations, cumulative year by year (WMO/OSCAR). The larger the network, the better the Pacific measures its own climate.
act8.regional_meteo_sub: Total operational stations across visible territories, year by year. The line rises: observing capacity strengthens.
act8.meteo_hm_sub: Number of stations by territory and year.
act8.meteo_rank_sub: Number of stations at the last available year; reference = median of visible territories.
act8.meteo_cmp_sub: Number of stations from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.rain_guide_intro: GPCP v2.3 data (NOAA), 2.5° grid, monthly totals aggregated to an annual total per country. The anomaly is the departure from the 1991–2020 reference mean (WMO standard).
act8.rain_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1991–2020 normal. Above = wetter year, below = drier year.
act8.rain_guide_s2_v: The min–max band shows the spread across islands; each value also carries a standard error (uncertainty) computed from the year's monthly rainfall.
act8.rain_guide_s3_v: In millimetres: −20 means 20 mm below the normal over the year.
act8.rain_guide_takeaway: Example: Wallis & Futuna saw −20.5 in 1998 (a dry year) then +15 in 2017 (a wet year).
act8.temp_guide_intro: NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 data, 5° grid, monthly land surface anomalies (at 2 m) aggregated to an annual mean per country. Reference: 1971–2000.
act8.temp_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1971–2000 normal. Above = warmer than reference, below = cooler.
act8.temp_guide_s2_v: A standard error accompanies each value; early years (19th century) rest on statistical reconstruction, so they are more uncertain.
act8.temp_guide_s3_v: In degrees Celsius: +0.8 °C = land surface 0.8 °C above the normal.
act8.temp_guide_takeaway: Example: at Tokelau the anomaly moves from −0.94 °C in 1890 to +0.83 °C in 2024 — substantial warming.
act8.meteo_guide_intro: WMO data, OSCAR database. Only operational stations are counted; the indicator adds 1 per active year of each station, between opening and closure (or today).
act8.meteo_guide_s1_v: We rebuild each territory's total by summing station types (PDH also provides the breakdown by type).
act8.meteo_guide_s2_v: Conservative filtering (excluding 'Silent' or 'Unknown' stations) may underestimate the network but keeps the data robust.
act8.meteo_guide_s3_v: The higher the count, the stronger the capacity to observe the climate — this is the response to the disruptions.
act8.meteo_guide_takeaway: Example: in Samoa, 1 station in 1889, 4 stations in 2025.

act9.title: The exposed economy
act9.lead: Behind the climate, whole economies. The tourism that keeps the islands alive, the electricity they produce, and green taxation — a lever still barely used.
act9.unavailable: Economic data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act9.credit: Sources: tourism UN Tourism (CC-BY) · electricity IMF/IRENA · taxation IMF/OECD. Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act9.tour_sub: Visitor arrivals (counts), source UN Tourism. An economic backbone of the islands — and among the first hit by cyclones, damaged reefs and global shocks.
act9.regional_tour_sub: Sum of arrivals across visible territories, year by year. The 2020–2021 crater reveals how fragile a heavily dependent model is.
act9.tour_rank_sub: Arrivals at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.tour_cmp_sub: Arrivals from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.power_sub: Electricity generation (GWh), source IMF/IRENA. Includes grid, off-grid and self-consumption by energy industries.
act9.regional_power_sub: Sum of generation across visible territories, year by year. Demand climbs: growing pressure on the energy mix.
act9.power_hm_sub: GWh by territory and year (row-normalised, log scale).
act9.power_rank_sub: Generation at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.power_cmp_sub: Generation from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.tax_sub: Environmental taxes as % of GDP (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources), source IMF/OECD. Very limited coverage: only a few countries report — that scarcity is the point.
act9.regional_tax_sub: Median of reporting territories, year by year. The line stays low: the fiscal climate lever is barely used.
act9.tax_sm_title: Each reporting territory, year by year
act9.tax_hm_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) by territory and year.
act9.tax_rank_sub: Peak environmental taxes (% of GDP) reached over the whole period; reference = median of peaks. Territories with no documented tax are omitted.
act9.tax_cmp_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) from the first to the last available year.
act9.tour_guide_intro: UN Tourism data (arrival statistics). A tourist = a visitor staying at least one night; the total also includes same-day excursionists.
act9.tour_guide_s1_v: Absolute counts: 200,000 = 200,000 arrivals in the year.
act9.tour_guide_s2_v: The sharp 2020–2021 drop (COVID) shows how these economies hang by a single thread.
act9.tour_guide_s3_v: Bleached reefs, eroded beaches, more frequent cyclones: the climate directly threatens tourism appeal.
act9.tour_guide_takeaway: Example: French Polynesia went from 201,000 arrivals (1997) to 261,400 (2022).
act9.power_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from IRENA. The total sums all technologies and grid-connection types per country-year.
act9.power_guide_s1_v: In gigawatt-hours (GWh): the energy produced over the year.
act9.power_guide_s2_v: Includes grid-connected, off-grid and electricity self-consumed by energy industries.
act9.power_guide_s3_v: Rising generation = rising demand, hence a transition challenge toward renewables (see Act 5).
act9.power_guide_takeaway: Example: Fiji rose from about 673 GWh (2000) to 1,285 GWh (2023).
act9.tax_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from the OECD. Only percentage-of-GDP observations are kept, broken down by tax type (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources).
act9.tax_guide_s1_v: As % of GDP: 0.89 means 0.89% of GDP comes from environmental taxes.
act9.tax_guide_s2_v: Extremely limited coverage (~44% missing; only a handful of countries). Few territories pull this lever.
act9.tax_guide_s3_v: The total is reliable only when all categories are documented for a country-year; we obtain it by summing the types.
act9.tax_guide_takeaway: Example: in Fiji, the weight of these taxes goes from 0.89% of GDP (2011) to 0.4% (2020).

act10.title: The body and the water
act10.lead: As close to people as it gets: the water they drink and the disease that stalks them. The last step before the synthesis — where climate meets daily health.
act10.unavailable: Health data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act10.credit: Sources: water WHO/UNICEF (JMP) · tuberculosis WHO (Global TB Report 2024). Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act10.water_sub: Share of the population using safely managed drinking water services (%), source WHO/UNICEF. A fragile resource: saltwater intrusion into atoll aquifers, droughts, networks destroyed by cyclones.
act10.regional_water_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, higher is better: the goal is 100%.
act10.water_hm_sub: % of population by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means higher access.
act10.water_rank_sub: % at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.water_cmp_sub: Access to safe water from the first to the last available year. Rising = progress.
act10.tb_sub: Tuberculosis incidence per 100,000 people, source WHO. A marker of vulnerability (poverty, crowding, nutrition): climate acts mostly indirectly, through displacement and shocks.
act10.regional_tb_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, lower is better: a decline is progress.
act10.tb_hm_sub: Cases / 100,000 by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means a heavier burden.
act10.tb_rank_sub: Cases / 100,000 at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.tb_cmp_sub: Incidence from the first to the last available year. Falling = improvement.
act10.water_guide_title: How to read — safe drinking water
act10.water_guide_intro: WHO/UNICEF data (Joint Monitoring Programme, JMP). A 'safely managed' service = water accessible at home, available when needed and free from contamination.
act10.water_guide_s1_v: As % of population: 95 means 95% of people have safe water.
act10.water_guide_s2_v: Higher is better. A rising line = progress; a falling one (as in Solomon Islands) = a retreat to watch.
act10.water_guide_s3_v: On atolls, rising seas salinise aquifers; droughts and cyclones cut access. Safe water is on the front line.
act10.water_guide_takeaway: Example: Papua New Guinea went from about 32% (2000) to 50% (2022), while Solomon Islands fell from 79% to 67%.
act10.tb_guide_intro: WHO data (Global Tuberculosis Report 2024). Incidence measures estimated new cases each year, relative to population.
act10.tb_guide_s1_v: As cases per 100,000 people: 100 = 100 new cases a year per 100,000 people.
act10.tb_guide_s2_v: Lower is better. A decline is progress. On the maps, a darker cell = a heavier burden.
act10.tb_guide_s3_v: For very small populations (Niue, Tokelau…), a single case sends the rate soaring: sharp swings there must be taken with caution. The climate link is indirect.
act10.tb_guide_takeaway: Example: Vanuatu cut its incidence from 110 (2000) to 41 (2023); conversely, it climbs in Kiribati (~355 → 533).

act11.title: Guilty of almost nothing, exposed to almost everything
act11.lead: Ten acts, one thread. Here we cross everything we've seen: what these islands emit, and what they bear. Responsibility on one side, vulnerability on the other — and the gap that lays bare the Pacific's climate injustice.
act11.unavailable: Synthesis data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act11.credit: Sources: Pacific Data Hub (SPC) — GHG/capita, sea level & SST (IMF, NOAA), precipitation (GPCP), drinking water (WHO/UNICEF), tuberculosis (WHO), Red List (IUCN).
act11.credit_partial: Some indicators were unavailable at load time: the index is computed on those that responded.
act11.scatter_sub: Each dot = a territory. Right: emits more CO₂ per capita. Up: more exposed to climate. Lines mark the medians (four quadrants). Colour = sub-region.
act11.rank_sub: Composite index (0–100, higher = more exposed) at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act11.profile_sub: Index broken down by dimension (0–100, 100 = the Pacific's worst on that dimension). Pick 'All' to return to the ranking.
act11.guide_title: How to read — the vulnerability index
act11.guide_intro: We bring together six signals seen in earlier acts — sea level, sea-temperature anomaly, rainfall deviation, drinking-water access, tuberculosis, Red List Index — for each territory, at their last known value.
act11.guide_s1_v: Each indicator is rescaled to 0–100 (the Pacific's worst = 100), with the right polarity: water and biodiversity are inverted (low = worse), rainfall counted as absolute deviation. The index is the equal-weight average of available dimensions.
act11.guide_s2_v: Right of the scatter = emits more per head; up = more exposed. The top-left quadrant gathers those who weigh little on the climate yet suffer it most — the heart of the injustice.
act11.guide_s3_v: This is not an official index: equal weights by choice, normalisation relative to the Pacific (not the world), volatile small populations, and a territory missing a dimension is averaged over the others. Read it as a narrative, not a verdict.
act11.guide_takeaway: The overall pattern fits in one line: the smallest emitters are rarely the least exposed — often the opposite.
act11.stat_inj_sub: territories below the world average AND above the vulnerability median
